prevalence.source: Indian Rheumatology Association (IRA) and ICMR surveys
prevalence.trend: increasing due to aging population and improved diagnostics

epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: ACR/EULAR 2010 criteria adapted for India, RF/anti-CCP testing where available

publicHealthSystem.controlRate: 30% poor due to limited access and late diagnosis

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: IRA/ACR criteria, clinical diagnosis common, limited RF/anti-CCP access in rural areas
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: DMARDs (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine), limited biologic access (high cost), Ayurvedic options (e.g., Rasna, Guggulu)
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Severe rheumatologist shortage (<1 per million), expensive biologics, late diagnosis, rural access barriers
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Affordable generic DMARDs, IRA guidelines, Ayurvedic anti-inflammatory herbs, growing biosimilar availability
